learning_objectives: Describe the security architecture of modern operating systems including kernel and user space separation
Explain hardware security features that support OS security including NX bit, ASLR, and SMEP
Identify the Trusted Computing Base and its role in system security
Compare monolithic, microkernel, and hybrid kernel architectures from a security perspective
readings: Operating System Concepts (Silberschatz) Ch. 1-2
NIST SP 800-123: Guide to General Server Security
Windows Internals Part 1 (Russinovich) Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: Operating systems are the foundation upon which all software runs, and their security mechanisms form the first and most critical line of defense against both local and remote attacks. The operating system mediates every interaction between software and hardware: it controls which processes can access which memory regions, which users can read which files, which network connections are permitted, and which system resources are available to each application. A compromise of the operating system grants the attacker the highest level of control over the system and everything running on it. In this opening session we examine OS architecture from a security perspective, starting with the fundamental separation between kernel mode and user mode. The kernel operates with unrestricted hardware access and is responsible for enforcing all security policies. User mode processes operate in a restricted environment where the CPU itself prevents direct hardware access, forcing all system interactions through controlled system call interfaces. We examine the hardware features that make this separation possible and the security mechanisms built on top of it, including Address Space Layout Randomization, Data Execution Prevention, and Supervisor Mode Execution Prevention. Understanding these architectural foundations is essential before we can discuss the specific security features of Windows and Linux in subsequent sessions.
key_concepts: Kernel mode vs user mode: CPU privilege rings, Ring 0 (kernel) has unrestricted access, Ring 3 (user) is restricted by hardware
System calls: the controlled interface between user mode and kernel mode, validated by the kernel before execution
Process isolation: each process has its own virtual address space, preventing one process from accessing another's memory
Memory protection: page tables with permission bits (read, write, execute) enforced by the MMU hardware
Address Space Layout Randomization (ASLR): randomizes memory addresses of stack, heap, and libraries to prevent exploitation
Data Execution Prevention (DEP/NX bit): marks data pages as non-executable, preventing code execution from buffer overflow payloads
Supervisor Mode Execution Prevention (SMEP): prevents kernel from executing user-mode code, blocking common privilege escalation techniques
Trusted Computing Base (TCB): the minimal set of hardware, firmware, and software components critical to security enforcement
Monolithic vs microkernel security: monolithic kernels have larger attack surface but better performance; microkernels minimize TCB but have IPC overhead
Secure boot: verifying the integrity of boot components from firmware through bootloader to kernel using cryptographic signatures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Demonstrating process isolation on Linux by attempting cross-process memory access and observing kernel enforcement, then using GDB to examine the memory layout of a process showing ASLR randomization across multiple executions, and verifying DEP by attempting to execute code from the stack
setup_requirements: Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS VM with development tools
Windows Server 2022 VM with debugging tools
GDB debugger with pwndbg extension installed
Sample C programs for memory layout demonstration

review.value: Kernel mode (Ring 0) has unrestricted hardware access. User mode (Ring 3) cannot directly access hardware, I/O ports, or other processes' memory. System calls are the controlled gateway: the kernel validates each request before executing it.

Address Space Layout Randomization randomizes the base addresses of the stack, heap, shared libraries, and executable. Without ASLR, an attacker can predict where code and data reside and craft reliable exploits. With ASLR, the attacker must first leak an address to calculate offsets.

Data Execution Prevention marks data memory pages as non-executable. Even if an attacker injects shellcode into a buffer, the CPU refuses to execute it because the page has the NX (No eXecute) bit set. Attackers bypass DEP using Return-Oriented Programming.

UEFI Secure Boot verifies each component in the boot chain using cryptographic signatures. The firmware verifies the bootloader, the bootloader verifies the kernel, and the kernel verifies drivers. A rootkit that modifies any boot component will fail signature verification.

The Trusted Computing Base should be as small as possible because any bug in the TCB is a security vulnerability. Microkernel architectures minimize TCB by running drivers and services in user mode. Monolithic kernels like Linux include drivers in kernel mode, expanding the TCB.

learning_objectives: Understand Windows security architecture including SAM, LSA, and LSASS
Configure Group Policy Objects for enterprise security hardening
Implement Windows Defender Application Control and AppLocker
Describe Active Directory security fundamentals and common attack vectors
readings: Windows Internals Part 1 Ch. 7: Security
CIS Benchmark for Windows Server 2022
Microsoft Security Baselines Documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Windows dominates enterprise computing environments, running on the vast majority of corporate workstations and a significant percentage of servers. Understanding Windows security architecture is therefore essential for any cybersecurity professional working in enterprise environments. The Windows security model centers on the Security Reference Monitor in the kernel, the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service that handles authentication and stores credentials, the Security Account Manager database that stores local account credentials, and Active Directory that provides centralized identity and access management for domain-joined systems. In this session we examine each component in depth, understanding both its intended function and its security weaknesses. LSASS is a prime target for credential dumping tools like Mimikatz because it holds authentication credentials in memory. The SAM database stores password hashes that can be extracted by tools with administrative access. Active Directory, while providing powerful centralized management, creates a high-value target where compromise of a domain controller grants the attacker control over every system in the domain. We examine the defensive tools Windows provides: Group Policy for enforcing security configurations across thousands of machines, AppLocker and Windows Defender Application Control for application whitelisting, Credential Guard for protecting LSASS from credential dumping, and Windows Defender for endpoint protection.
key_concepts: Security Account Manager (SAM): stores local user account credentials as NTLM hashes, accessible via SYSTEM privilege
Local Security Authority Subsystem (LSASS): handles authentication, stores credentials in memory, and enforces security policies
Active Directory security: domain controllers, Kerberos authentication, Group Policy distribution, and trust relationships
Group Policy Objects (GPOs): centralized configuration management for password policies, audit settings, software restrictions, and security settings
AppLocker: application whitelisting based on publisher, path, or file hash to prevent execution of unauthorized software
Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC): kernel-enforced application control using Code Integrity policies
Credential Guard: uses virtualization-based security to isolate LSASS in a secure container inaccessible to admin-level malware
Windows Event Log: Security, System, and Application channels for audit logging with configurable advanced audit policies
BitLocker: full disk encryption using TPM for protecting data at rest, with network unlock and recovery key management
Windows Firewall with Advanced Security: host-based firewall with inbound, outbound, and connection security rules
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring AppLocker policies to whitelist only signed Microsoft applications and blocking execution of unsigned binaries, then demonstrating Credential Guard by showing that Mimikatz cannot extract credentials from the protected LSASS process, comparing with an unprotected system where extraction succeeds
setup_requirements: Windows Server 2022 with Active Directory configured
Windows 10/11 client VM joined to the domain
Group Policy Management Console installed
Mimikatz binary for credential extraction demonstration

review.value: LSASS stores authentication credentials in memory: NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and potentially plaintext passwords (if WDigest is enabled). Mimikatz reads LSASS memory to extract these credentials. Defense: Credential Guard isolates LSASS using virtualization-based security.

AppLocker runs in user mode and can be bypassed by certain techniques. WDAC runs in kernel mode using Code Integrity, making it much harder to bypass. WDAC is recommended for high-security environments; AppLocker is suitable for general application management.

GPOs can enforce: password policy (minimum length, complexity, history), account lockout policy, audit policy (log logons, privilege use, object access), software restriction, and firewall rules. Applied to OUs and inherited unless blocked. Essential for consistent security across the enterprise.

Kerberoasting: requesting service tickets for service accounts and cracking them offline. Pass-the-Hash: using stolen NTLM hashes to authenticate without knowing the password. Golden Ticket: forging Kerberos TGTs with a compromised KRBTGT hash for persistent domain access.

learning_objectives: Configure Linux file permissions, ownership, and special permission bits
Implement SELinux mandatory access controls and create custom policies
Harden a Linux system using CIS benchmark guidelines
Manage user accounts, groups, and privilege escalation securely
readings: The Linux Command Line (Shotts) Ch. 9-10: Permissions
CIS Benchmark for Ubuntu Linux 22.04
Red Hat SELinux User Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Linux powers the majority of servers on the internet, runs virtually every cloud platform, and underpins most security tools and appliances. Mastering Linux security is therefore essential for any cybersecurity professional. The Linux security model builds on decades of Unix tradition, implementing discretionary access controls through file permissions and ownership, mandatory access controls through frameworks like SELinux and AppArmor, and a sophisticated system of capabilities that allow fine-grained privilege assignment without resorting to full root access. In this session we examine the Linux permission model in depth, starting with the standard read-write-execute permissions for owner, group, and others, and then covering the special permission bits SUID, SGID, and the sticky bit that create both powerful functionality and dangerous security risks. SUID binaries are a perennial source of privilege escalation vulnerabilities: a misconfigured SUID binary owned by root gives any user who can execute it the ability to run code with root privileges. We then examine mandatory access controls with SELinux, which assigns security contexts to every process and file, enforcing policy even against processes running as root. SELinux represents a fundamental shift from the traditional Unix model where root has unrestricted access: under SELinux, even root is constrained by policy. We cover the practical skills of managing SELinux modes, troubleshooting policy denials, and creating custom policy modules for applications.
key_concepts: Linux permission model: read (4), write (2), execute (1) for owner, group, others; octal and symbolic notation
Special permissions: SUID (execute as file owner), SGID (execute as file group), sticky bit (restrict deletion in shared directories)
SUID security risk: SUID root binaries allow privilege escalation if they can be exploited; audit all SUID binaries regularly
User and group management: useradd, usermod, groupadd, /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/group, and PAM configuration
sudo configuration: /etc/sudoers with least-privilege rules, NOPASSWD risks, and sudoers.d directory for modular configuration
SELinux: Mandatory Access Control using security contexts (user:role:type:level), enforcing policy independent of DAC permissions
SELinux modes: Enforcing (policy enforced), Permissive (policy violations logged but not enforced), Disabled (no SELinux)
SELinux troubleshooting: ausearch for denial messages, audit2why for human-readable explanations, audit2allow for policy generation
AppArmor: profile-based MAC using pathname-based access control, simpler than SELinux but less granular
Linux capabilities: fine-grained privileges (CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE, CAP_SYS_ADMIN) replacing the all-or-nothing root model
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring SELinux in enforcing mode on CentOS, demonstrating how a web server process is confined to its designated file paths, creating a custom SELinux policy module for a new application using audit2allow, and showing how SELinux prevents a simulated privilege escalation even when the attacker gains root shell access
setup_requirements: CentOS/RHEL VM with SELinux in enforcing mode
Ubuntu VM with AppArmor enabled
audit2allow, semanage, and restorecon tools
SUID binary examples for privilege escalation demonstration

review.value: When the SUID bit is set on an executable owned by root, any user executing it runs the program as root. If the program has a vulnerability (buffer overflow, command injection), the attacker gains root access. Always audit: find / -perm -4000 -type f and remove SUID from unnecessary binaries.

Even if an attacker gains root access through an exploit, SELinux confines the compromised process to its security context. A web server running as httpd_t can only access files labeled httpd_sys_content_t, even if the process UID is root. This is the power of mandatory access control.

Never use ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL. Instead, grant specific commands: michael ALL=(root) /usr/bin/systemctl restart httpd. Use /etc/sudoers.d/ for modular configuration. Always use visudo to edit. Log all sudo usage for audit trails.

Instead of making a binary SUID root, assign only the needed capability. Example: setcap cap_net_bind_service+ep /usr/bin/myapp allows the app to bind to port 80 without running as root. This follows least privilege by granting only the specific power needed.

learning_objectives: Implement secure user lifecycle management on Windows and Linux
Configure password policies and account lockout settings
Design role-based access schemes using groups and organizational units
Automate user provisioning and deprovisioning processes
readings: Operating System Concepts Ch. 14: Protection and Security
NIST SP 800-63B: Digital Identity Guidelines
Active Directory Administration Guide: User and Group Management

lecture_notes.introduction: User account management is where security policy meets daily operations. Every security framework begins with identity: knowing who is accessing the system, what they are authorized to do, and tracking what they actually do. Poor user management practices create some of the most common and most exploitable security weaknesses in organizations. Orphaned accounts from departed employees provide unauthorized access paths. Shared accounts eliminate accountability and complicate forensics. Excessive permissions violate least privilege and expand the blast radius of compromised credentials. Default accounts with known passwords provide easy initial access for attackers. In this session we examine the complete user lifecycle from creation through access assignment to deprovisioning, on both Windows and Linux systems. We discuss password policy configuration, examining how NIST SP 800-63B has revolutionized password guidance by recommending length over complexity, eliminating mandatory rotation, and emphasizing breached password screening. We implement group-based access control that maps organizational roles to permissions, making access management scalable and auditable. We explore the automation of user provisioning and deprovisioning through scripts and identity management tools, because manual processes inevitably leave orphaned accounts and inconsistent permissions.
key_concepts: User lifecycle: creation, access provisioning, periodic review, modification, and deprovisioning upon role change or departure
Windows user management: Active Directory Users and Computers, organizational units, user templates, and automated provisioning
Linux user management: useradd, usermod, userdel, /etc/passwd structure, /etc/shadow hash storage, and /etc/login.defs defaults
Password policy implementation: minimum length, complexity requirements, history, lockout thresholds, and NIST-recommended approaches
Account lockout: configuring lockout thresholds, duration, and observation windows to balance security with usability
Group-based access control: security groups map to roles, users assigned to groups, permissions assigned to groups not individual users
Organizational Units in Active Directory: hierarchical containers for applying Group Policy and delegating administration
Service accounts: dedicated accounts for application services with managed passwords, restricted logon rights, and minimal permissions
Privileged access management: time-limited, just-in-time elevated access with full session recording and approval workflows
Account auditing: regular access reviews, detection of dormant accounts, and automated deprovisioning triggers from HR systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Creating a group-based access control structure in Active Directory with organizational units for departments, security groups for roles, and GPOs for policy enforcement, then scripting automated user provisioning in PowerShell that creates the user, assigns groups, sets initial password, and enables MFA
setup_requirements: Windows Server 2022 Active Directory environment
Linux VMs with local user management
PowerShell scripting environment
User provisioning checklist and templates

review.value: Accounts of former employees that remain active are a top attack vector. The 2021 SolarWinds attack used a dormant service account. Mitigation: automate deprovisioning triggered by HR termination events, conduct quarterly access reviews, and disable accounts that have not logged in for 90 days.

NIST SP 800-63B recommends: minimum 8 characters (longer is better), no complexity composition rules, no mandatory rotation, check against breached password databases, support up to 64 characters, and allow paste into password fields. This is a dramatic departure from traditional policy.

Service accounts should: use Managed Service Accounts (gMSA) for automatic password rotation, be denied interactive logon, have the minimum permissions required, be monitored for unusual activity, and never be shared between services.

Instead of assigning permissions to User A and User B individually, create a Finance-Analysts group with required permissions, add both users to the group. When User A leaves, remove from group. When new analyst starts, add to group. Scalable, auditable, consistent.

learning_objectives: Configure NTFS permissions and inheritance on Windows systems
Implement ext4 permissions, ACLs, and file attributes on Linux
Design file system security for shared resources in enterprise environments
Detect and respond to unauthorized file access and modifications
readings: Windows Server Administration: NTFS Permissions Guide
Linux System Administration: File System Security
CIS Controls: Data Protection

lecture_notes.introduction: File systems are where data lives, and file system permissions are the mechanism that determines who can read, modify, or execute that data. On both Windows and Linux, the file system permission model is rich and complex, supporting granular access control that, when properly configured, enforces the principle of least privilege at the data level. However, this complexity also means that misconfigurations are common and can create significant security vulnerabilities. In this session we examine file system security on both major platforms. On Windows, NTFS provides a sophisticated permission model with thirteen granular permissions that can be combined into standard permission sets, with inheritance flowing from parent folders to child objects. Understanding NTFS inheritance, explicit versus inherited permissions, and the interaction between share permissions and NTFS permissions is essential for securing Windows file servers. On Linux, the traditional owner/group/other permission model is extended by POSIX Access Control Lists that allow fine-grained per-user and per-group permissions beyond the basic three-entity model. Linux also provides file attributes like immutable (i) and append-only (a) that add additional protection layers. We examine encryption at the file system level with Windows EFS and Linux eCryptfs/fscrypt, and discuss file integrity monitoring tools that detect unauthorized modifications.
key_concepts: NTFS permissions: Full Control, Modify, Read and Execute, Read, Write, and 13 granular special permissions
NTFS inheritance: permissions flow from parent folders to child objects, can be blocked or explicitly overridden
Share permissions vs NTFS permissions: the most restrictive of the two applies when accessing files over the network
Linux standard permissions: rwx for owner, group, others; umask for default permission settings on new files
POSIX ACLs on Linux: getfacl and setfacl for per-user and per-group permissions beyond the basic owner/group/other model
Linux file attributes: chattr +i for immutable (cannot be modified even by root), chattr +a for append-only (for log files)
File system encryption: BitLocker (volume-level), EFS (file-level) on Windows; LUKS (volume-level), fscrypt (directory-level) on Linux
File integrity monitoring: AIDE and Tripwire on Linux, comparing current file hashes against a known-good baseline database
Data classification enforcement: mapping data classification levels to file system permissions and encryption requirements
Auditing file access: Windows SACL (System Access Control List) and Linux auditd rules for tracking file read, write, and delete operations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring a shared file server on Windows with department folders using NTFS permissions and inheritance, then setting up AIDE on Linux to create a baseline database and detect unauthorized modifications to system configuration files, demonstrating alert generation when a critical file is modified
setup_requirements: Windows Server with file sharing configured
Linux VM with AIDE or Tripwire installed
Multiple user accounts with different group memberships
File integrity monitoring baseline database

review.value: By default, permissions on a parent folder propagate to all child folders and files. Explicit permissions override inherited ones. Breaking inheritance at a subfolder creates an independent ACL. Best practice: set permissions at the top level and let inheritance propagate.

chattr +i /etc/resolv.conf makes the file immutable: it cannot be modified, deleted, renamed, or linked even by root. Only root can remove the attribute with chattr -i. Useful for protecting critical configuration files from accidental or malicious modification.

AIDE creates a database of file hashes, permissions, and attributes for monitored directories. Running aide --check compares current state against the baseline. Any modification to a monitored file generates an alert. Essential for detecting rootkits and unauthorized configuration changes.

Share permissions apply only to network access. NTFS permissions apply to both local and network access. The effective permission is the most restrictive combination. Best practice: set share permissions to Full Control for Authenticated Users, then use NTFS permissions for granular access control.

learning_objectives: Monitor running processes on Windows and Linux for security threats
Identify malicious processes through behavioral analysis and system call monitoring
Configure process-level security controls and sandboxing
Investigate suspicious process activity using forensic tools
readings: Operating System Concepts Ch. 3-4: Processes and Threads
Windows Sysinternals Administrator's Reference: Process Explorer and Process Monitor
Linux Process Monitoring and Security

lecture_notes.introduction: Processes are the fundamental unit of execution on any operating system, and monitoring process activity is one of the most powerful techniques for detecting malicious behavior. Every action an attacker takes on a compromised system manifests as process activity: malware execution creates new processes, privilege escalation modifies process tokens, persistence mechanisms create scheduled tasks or services, and data exfiltration generates network connections from processes. Understanding how to monitor, analyze, and control process behavior is essential for both real-time threat detection and forensic investigation. In this session we examine process management and security on both Windows and Linux. On Windows, we use the Sysinternals tools Process Explorer and Process Monitor to observe process creation, parent-child relationships, loaded DLLs, network connections, and registry access. On Linux, we use ps, top, htop, lsof, and strace to examine running processes, their resource usage, open files, and system call activity. We learn to identify suspicious indicators: processes running from unusual locations, processes with unexpected parent processes, processes making network connections to external IP addresses, and processes accessing sensitive files they should not touch. We also examine process-level security controls including Windows Job Objects, Linux cgroups and namespaces, and application sandboxing techniques.
key_concepts: Process attributes: PID, PPID, user context, priority, memory usage, CPU usage, open files, and network connections
Process tree analysis: examining parent-child relationships to detect anomalies like cmd.exe spawned by Microsoft Word
Windows Process Explorer: real-time process monitoring with DLL inspection, handle listing, and VirusTotal integration
Windows Process Monitor: system call tracing for file, registry, and network operations with filtering for targeted analysis
Linux process monitoring: ps aux, top, htop for process listing; lsof for open files; strace for system call tracing
Suspicious process indicators: running from temp directories, unusual parent processes, hidden processes, and unexpected network activity
Process injection techniques: DLL injection, process hollowing, and thread hijacking used by malware to hide in legitimate processes
Windows Job Objects and Linux cgroups: resource limits and isolation for controlling process resource consumption
Linux namespaces: process, network, mount, and user namespaces for process isolation, the foundation of container technology
Application sandboxing: restricting process capabilities to minimize the impact of exploitation using AppArmor profiles or seccomp filters
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using Process Explorer on Windows to identify a suspicious process spawned by a macro-enabled document, tracing its parent-child chain, examining its DLLs and network connections, then using strace on Linux to monitor system calls of a suspicious process and identify malicious file access and network communication
setup_requirements: Windows VM with Sysinternals Suite installed
Linux VM with strace, ltrace, and lsof available
Simulated malicious processes for analysis exercises
Process monitoring baseline documentation

review.value: Normal: explorer.exe spawns chrome.exe. Suspicious: winword.exe spawns cmd.exe spawns powershell.exe. This chain indicates a macro executing system commands, a classic indicator of malicious document exploitation.

strace -p PID traces system calls of a running process. strace -e network shows only network-related calls. strace -e open shows file access. Essential for understanding what a suspicious process is doing without disassembling the binary.

Malware injects code into legitimate processes (explorer.exe, svchost.exe) to hide its activity. Detection: look for processes with unexpected DLLs loaded, unexpected memory regions marked as executable, or network connections from processes that normally do not communicate externally.

cgroups limit resource usage (CPU, memory, I/O) for a process group. Namespaces isolate what a process can see (PID namespace: only its own processes; network namespace: its own network stack). Together they form the foundation of container isolation.

learning_objectives: Identify unnecessary services and disable them on Windows and Linux
Harden critical network services including SSH, web servers, and database servers
Configure service accounts with minimum required privileges
Implement service isolation using systemd security directives
readings: NIST SP 800-123: Guide to General Server Security
CIS Benchmark Sections on Service Configuration
systemd.exec Security Directives Documentation

lecture_notes.introduction: Every running service is a potential attack surface. A service listening on a network port accepts incoming connections from anyone who can reach it, and any vulnerability in that service can be exploited remotely. Even services that only listen locally expand the local attack surface, potentially enabling privilege escalation from a low-privileged compromise. The principle of minimal services states that a system should run only the services required for its designated function, and those services should be configured with the minimum privileges necessary. In this session we examine service hardening from both a Windows and Linux perspective. We begin by auditing running services to identify unnecessary ones, then systematically harden the services that must remain. For SSH, we configure key-based authentication, disable root login, restrict allowed users, and enable fail2ban for brute force protection. For web servers, we configure TLS properly, remove default content, set restrictive permissions, and enable security headers. For all services, we apply the principle of least privilege through service account configuration and systemd security directives that provide sandboxing capabilities including read-only file systems, restricted network access, private /tmp directories, and capability dropping. These directives represent one of the most powerful and underutilized security features in modern Linux.
key_concepts: Attack surface reduction: disabling unnecessary services, closing unnecessary ports, removing unnecessary software
Windows service hardening: service account configuration, service permissions, Windows Firewall rules per service
Linux service management: systemctl for service lifecycle, systemd unit files for configuration and security
SSH hardening: key-based authentication, PasswordAuthentication no, PermitRootLogin no, AllowUsers directive, Port change
fail2ban: monitors log files for repeated authentication failures and temporarily blocks offending IP addresses
Web server hardening: TLS 1.3 configuration, HSTS header, server version hiding, directory listing disabled, security headers
Database hardening: bind to localhost only, strong authentication, encrypted connections, least-privilege database users
systemd security directives: ProtectSystem=strict, PrivateTmp=yes, NoNewPrivileges=yes, CapabilityBoundingSet=
Service account isolation: dedicated user per service, no login shell, restricted home directory, minimal group membership
Service monitoring: tracking service restarts, unexpected service additions, and unauthorized service modifications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Hardening an SSH server by implementing key-based authentication, disabling password login, restricting to specific users, configuring fail2ban, then applying systemd security directives to an Apache web server to create a read-only filesystem sandbox, private /tmp, and dropped capabilities, verifying with systemd-analyze security
setup_requirements: Linux VM with multiple services running
SSH key pair generation tools
fail2ban installed and configured
systemd-analyze security for service security scoring

review.value: Key-based auth only (PasswordAuthentication no), no root login (PermitRootLogin no), restrict users (AllowUsers), use protocol 2 only, set MaxAuthTries 3, enable logging, consider non-standard port, implement fail2ban, use strong key types (Ed25519).

Run systemd-analyze security servicename to get a security exposure score from 0.0 (fully locked down) to 10.0 (no security). Each unset security directive contributes to the score. Target score below 5.0 for production services.

When NoNewPrivileges=yes is set in a systemd unit, the service process and all its children cannot gain new privileges through SUID binaries, capabilities, or any other mechanism. This is a critical defense against privilege escalation from a compromised service.

Regularly audit running services: systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running on Linux, Get-Service | Where-Object Status -eq Running on Windows. Compare against an approved service list. Any unauthorized service warrants investigation.

learning_objectives: Navigate and secure the Windows Registry for security hardening
Identify registry-based persistence mechanisms used by malware
Secure Linux configuration files and detect unauthorized modifications
Implement configuration management for consistent security baselines
readings: Windows Registry Forensics (Carvey) Ch. 1-4
CIS Benchmark: Registry Security Settings
Linux System Configuration Security Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database that stores configuration settings for the operating system, applications, and user preferences. From a security perspective, the Registry is critically important for two reasons: it contains security-relevant settings that control system behavior, and it is one of the primary persistence mechanisms used by malware. Understanding the Registry's structure, the security-critical keys, and the common persistence techniques attackers use is essential for both hardening and incident response. On the Linux side, configuration is distributed across text files primarily in /etc, providing a different but equally important set of hardening targets. In this session we explore both platforms' configuration systems from a security perspective. On Windows, we navigate the five root keys (HKLM, HKCU, HKCR, HKU, HKCC), examine security-critical settings in the Security and SAM hives, and learn to identify the Run/RunOnce keys, Winlogon, and service entries where malware commonly establishes persistence. We configure registry permissions to prevent unauthorized modification and implement registry auditing to detect changes. On Linux, we secure critical configuration files in /etc, implement file integrity monitoring for configuration directories, and use configuration management tools to enforce consistent baselines across multiple systems.
key_concepts: Windows Registry structure: five root keys, hives (SAM, SECURITY, SOFTWARE, SYSTEM), and values (REG_SZ, REG_DWORD, REG_BINARY)
Registry persistence locations: Run, RunOnce, Winlogon Shell/Userinit, Services, Scheduled Tasks, Browser Helper Objects
Registry security hardening: restricting permissions on security-critical keys, enabling registry auditing via SACL
Security Policy registry settings: LanManager authentication level, SMB signing, UAC configuration, and credential caching
Linux configuration files: /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, /etc/sudoers, /etc/ssh/sshd_config, /etc/fstab security options
Linux persistence mechanisms: cron jobs, systemd services, .bashrc/.profile modifications, authorized_keys additions
Configuration management: Ansible, Puppet, or Chef for enforcing consistent security configurations across systems
Configuration drift detection: comparing current configuration against approved baseline and alerting on deviations
Security-relevant sysctl settings: network stack hardening, ICMP redirect disabling, source routing prevention
fstab security options: noexec (no execution), nosuid (ignore SUID bits), nodev (no device files) for mounted file systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Examining the Windows Registry for common malware persistence locations using Autoruns from Sysinternals, then demonstrating how malware adds a Run key entry for persistence and how to detect it, followed by using Ansible to enforce sysctl hardening settings across multiple Linux VMs and verifying compliance
setup_requirements: Windows VM with Sysinternals Autoruns installed
Registry editor and PowerShell for registry manipulation
Linux VMs with Ansible configured for configuration management
Baseline configuration files for comparison

review.value: HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run: entries here execute automatically when the user logs in. Malware commonly adds entries pointing to dropped payloads. Autoruns from Sysinternals shows all autostart locations including Run keys with VirusTotal integration.

net.ipv4.ip_forward=0 (disable routing), net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects=0 (ignore ICMP redirects), net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1 (SYN flood protection), kernel.randomize_va_space=2 (full ASLR). Apply via /etc/sysctl.conf and verify with sysctl -a.

Ansible playbooks define the desired state (packages installed, services running, files present with correct permissions). Running the playbook enforces that state. Any configuration drift is automatically corrected. This ensures all systems maintain the security baseline.

Mount /tmp with noexec,nosuid,nodev to prevent execution of binaries, SUID exploitation, and device file creation in the temp directory. Mount /var/log with append-only to protect log integrity. These mount options are defense-in-depth measures against common attack techniques.

learning_objectives: Configure comprehensive audit logging on Windows and Linux systems
Analyze system logs to identify security events and indicators of compromise
Implement centralized log management with integrity protection
Design an audit policy that balances security visibility with storage and performance
readings: NIST SP 800-92: Guide to Computer Security Log Management
Linux auditd Documentation and Rules Reference
Windows Advanced Audit Policy Configuration Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Logs are the evidence trail of system activity, and without proper logging and monitoring, breaches go undetected for an average of 277 days according to the IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report. Logging is not merely a compliance checkbox; it is the foundation of security monitoring, incident detection, forensic investigation, and accountability. Every security-relevant action on a system should be logged: user logins, privilege escalation, file access, process creation, network connections, policy changes, and system modifications. In this session we configure comprehensive audit logging on both Windows and Linux, understanding what events to capture, how to configure the logging subsystems, and how to analyze the resulting logs. On Windows, we configure Advanced Audit Policies through Group Policy, enabling detailed logging of logon events, privilege use, object access, and policy changes. On Linux, we configure the auditd framework with custom rules that monitor file access to sensitive files, track process execution, and detect privilege escalation. We discuss centralized log management using syslog, examining how to forward logs to a central server for aggregation, correlation, and protection from tampering. We address log integrity: if an attacker can modify or delete logs, the entire audit trail is compromised. Protections include remote logging, append-only storage, log signing, and WORM (Write Once Read Many) storage.
key_concepts: Windows Advanced Audit Policy: nine categories including Account Logon, Logon/Logoff, Object Access, Privilege Use, and Policy Change
Critical Windows Event IDs: 4624 (logon), 4625 (failed logon), 4672 (admin logon), 4720 (user created), 4732 (user added to group)
Linux auditd: kernel-level audit framework with rules for monitoring system calls, file access, and user commands
auditd rule syntax: -w /etc/shadow -p wa -k shadow_access watches the shadow file for write and attribute changes
aureport and ausearch: tools for querying and reporting on audit logs by event type, user, time, and key
Syslog and rsyslog: centralized log forwarding protocol, facility and severity levels, and remote logging configuration
Log integrity protection: remote logging (attacker cannot delete remote copies), append-only storage, log signing with hash chains
Security-relevant events to monitor: authentication events, privilege changes, file access to sensitive data, process creation, and network connections
Log retention policies: regulatory requirements (HIPAA 6 years, PCI-DSS 1 year), storage planning, and archival strategies
Log analysis techniques: pattern matching, baseline comparison, anomaly detection, and correlation across multiple log sources
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring auditd rules to detect unauthorized access to /etc/shadow, forwarding audit logs to a central rsyslog server, then performing a simulated attack and using aureport and ausearch to trace the complete attack chain from initial access through privilege escalation to data access
setup_requirements: Linux VM with auditd configured
Windows VM with Advanced Audit Policies enabled
Central rsyslog server for log aggregation
Sample attack scenarios for log analysis exercises

review.value: -w /etc/shadow -p rwa -k shadow_file: watches for read, write, and attribute changes to the shadow file. -a always,exit -S execve -k command_log: logs every command execution. These rules create a comprehensive audit trail for security investigation.

Failed logon attempt. Track failure reasons: 0xC000006A (wrong password), 0xC0000234 (account locked), 0xC0000072 (account disabled). Multiple 4625 events from the same source indicate brute force. Multiple failures across many accounts indicate password spraying.

Forward logs to a remote server that the attacker cannot access. Even if the attacker gains root and clears local logs, the remote copies survive. Configure rsyslog: *.* @@logserver.company.local:514 sends all logs to the remote server via TCP.

Start with authentication failures (brute force detection). Then new account creation and privilege changes (persistence). Then unusual process execution (malware). Then sensitive file access (data theft). Then network connections to unusual destinations (C2 and exfiltration).

learning_objectives: Design an enterprise patch management lifecycle and strategy
Use vulnerability scanners to identify missing patches and prioritize remediation
Implement automated patching with WSUS, SCCM, and Ansible
Balance patching urgency with change management and operational stability
readings: NIST SP 800-40 Rev. 4: Guide to Enterprise Patch Management
CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog
CVSS v3.1 Specification Document

lecture_notes.introduction: Unpatched systems are consistently among the top initial access vectors for successful cyberattacks. The window between vulnerability disclosure and active exploitation continues to shrink, with some vulnerabilities being exploited within hours of disclosure. Yet patching in enterprise environments is far more complex than simply clicking update. Production systems run business-critical applications that may break with OS updates. Change management processes require testing and approval before modifications. Maintenance windows are limited. And the sheer volume of patches, with Microsoft alone releasing 50 to 100 patches per month, makes prioritization essential. In this session we examine patch management as a structured lifecycle: vulnerability identification through scanning, risk-based prioritization using CVSS scores and exploitation intelligence, testing in a staging environment, deployment through automated tools, verification of successful installation, and rollback procedures for patches that cause problems. We discuss the tools that enable enterprise-scale patching: WSUS and SCCM for Windows environments, and Ansible or similar configuration management tools for Linux. We examine how to use vulnerability scanners like Nessus and OpenVAS to identify missing patches, and how to prioritize remediation using the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, CVSS scores, and asset criticality.
key_concepts: Patch management lifecycle: identify, prioritize, test, deploy, verify, and document for every patch cycle
CVSS scoring: base, temporal, and environmental scores for vulnerability prioritization on a 0-10 scale
CISA KEV catalog: list of vulnerabilities known to be actively exploited, requiring priority remediation
WSUS: Windows Server Update Services for centralized Windows patch distribution with approval workflows
SCCM/MECM: Microsoft Endpoint Configuration Manager for comprehensive patch management with compliance reporting
Ansible for Linux patching: playbooks for automated package updates across multiple systems with pre and post-update checks
Vulnerability scanning: Nessus, OpenVAS/GVM for identifying missing patches with CVSS-scored findings
Patch prioritization matrix: combining CVSS score, exploitation status, asset criticality, and exposure to determine urgency
Testing and staging: validating patches against representative systems before production deployment to prevent outages
Rollback procedures: system snapshots, backup verification, and documented rollback steps for every patch deployment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running an OpenVAS vulnerability scan against a deliberately unpatched Windows and Linux VM, analyzing the results to prioritize remediation using CVSS scores and CISA KEV status, applying patches using WSUS and Ansible, then rescanning to verify remediation and demonstrate reduced vulnerability count
setup_requirements: OpenVAS/GVM scanner installed and configured
Intentionally unpatched Windows and Linux VMs as scan targets
WSUS server for Windows patch deployment
Ansible playbook for Linux patch automation

review.value: Critical (9.0-10.0): patch within 7 days, compensating controls immediately. High (7.0-8.9): patch within 30 days. Medium (4.0-6.9): patch within 90 days. Low (0.1-3.9): patch in next scheduled cycle. CISA KEV entries override these timelines: patch within 14 days regardless of CVSS.

When a zero-day is disclosed with no patch available, implement compensating controls: WAF rules, firewall restrictions, disabling vulnerable features, or removing affected software. Monitor CISA advisories and vendor communications for patch availability. Deploy emergency patch within 24-48 hours of release.

Never deploy patches directly to production. Test on staging systems that mirror production: same applications, same configurations. Run functional tests after patching. Verify critical business applications work. Only after successful staging testing deploy to production in a maintenance window.

Generate monthly patch compliance reports showing: total systems, patched percentage, overdue critical patches, time-to-patch metrics, and trend over time. Target 95%+ compliance within SLA timelines. Report to management with risk assessment for non-compliant systems.

learning_objectives: Apply CIS Benchmarks and DISA STIGs to harden operating systems
Automate compliance checking using scanning tools and scripts
Develop custom security baselines tailored to organizational requirements
Maintain ongoing compliance through continuous monitoring and remediation
readings: CIS Benchmark for Ubuntu Linux 22.04 (selected sections)
DISA STIG for Windows Server 2022 (selected sections)
NIST SP 800-128: Guide for Security-Focused Configuration Management

lecture_notes.introduction: Security baselines provide the standardized, documented configurations that serve as the foundation for a secure operating environment. Without baselines, every system is configured differently, security gaps are unpredictable and undetectable, and compliance audits become impossible. CIS Benchmarks and DISA STIGs are the two most widely used baseline standards, providing detailed configuration checklists for virtually every operating system and major application. CIS Benchmarks are developed by consensus among security professionals and offer two implementation levels: Level 1 for general use and Level 2 for high-security environments. DISA STIGs are developed by the Defense Information Systems Agency for U.S. Department of Defense systems, providing the most stringent hardening requirements. In this session we work through the process of applying security baselines to both Windows and Linux systems. We examine the CIS Benchmark structure, understand the rationale behind each recommendation, and use automated tools to assess compliance and apply configurations. We discuss the challenge of balancing security with functionality: some baseline recommendations may break applications or reduce usability, requiring documented exceptions with compensating controls. We implement continuous compliance monitoring that checks system configurations against the baseline on a regular schedule and alerts on deviations.
key_concepts: CIS Benchmarks: consensus-based security configuration guides with Level 1 (general) and Level 2 (high-security) recommendations
DISA STIGs: Security Technical Implementation Guides for DoD systems, more stringent than CIS with CAT I, II, III severity levels
CIS-CAT Pro: automated assessment tool that scans systems against CIS Benchmarks and generates compliance reports
SCAP (Security Content Automation Protocol): standardized format for expressing security configuration checklists, enabling automated assessment
OpenSCAP: open-source SCAP scanner for automated compliance checking against CIS and STIG profiles on Linux
Baseline exceptions: documenting and approving deviations from the baseline with compensating controls and risk acceptance
Continuous compliance monitoring: scheduled scans comparing current configuration against baseline with drift alerting
Configuration as Code: defining security baselines in Ansible playbooks or Chef cookbooks for automated, repeatable application
Audit readiness: maintaining evidence of baseline compliance for regulatory audits (PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOX)
Custom baseline development: combining CIS/STIG recommendations with organization-specific requirements based on risk assessment
lecture_notes.demonstration: Running OpenSCAP against an Ubuntu VM to assess CIS Benchmark Level 2 compliance, reviewing the HTML report to identify failing controls, remediating the top ten failures, then rescanning to show improved compliance score, followed by automating the remaining remediations in an Ansible playbook
setup_requirements: Ubuntu VM with OpenSCAP and CIS content installed
Windows VM with CIS-CAT Pro or STIG Viewer
Ansible for automated remediation
Baseline compliance report template

review.value: Level 1: practical hardening that can be applied to most systems with minimal impact on functionality. Example: require password minimum length of 14 characters. Level 2: deeper hardening for high-security systems that may impact usability. Example: disable USB storage devices, enable full audit logging.

CAT I (Critical): exploitation could lead to loss of confidentiality, integrity, or availability of classified/unclassified information. CAT II (Medium): could lead to degradation of security measures. CAT III (Low): could degrade measures protecting availability. CAT I findings must be remediated immediately.

Systems drift from baseline over time due to: manual changes during troubleshooting, application installations, configuration modifications not going through change management. Continuous monitoring detects drift; Configuration as Code automatically corrects it.

Some baseline requirements may break specific applications. Document: which control, why it cannot be applied, what compensating control is in place, who approved the exception, and when it will be reviewed. Never silently ignore a baseline failure.

learning_objectives: Identify security concerns specific to container environments
Harden Docker containers using security best practices and CIS Docker Benchmark
Implement container image scanning and supply chain security
Configure runtime security controls including seccomp profiles and AppArmor
readings: NIST SP 800-190: Application Container Security Guide
Docker Security Best Practices Documentation
CIS Docker Benchmark

lecture_notes.introduction: Containers have become the dominant deployment model for modern applications, and understanding their security model is essential for any OS security professional. Containers provide process-level isolation using Linux namespaces and cgroups, sharing the host kernel rather than running their own operating system like virtual machines. This sharing creates both efficiency and security trade-offs: containers start faster and use fewer resources, but the shared kernel means that a kernel vulnerability can potentially affect all containers on the host. Container security encompasses the entire lifecycle: securing the base images, scanning for vulnerabilities, building with minimal attack surface, configuring runtime security controls, and monitoring container behavior for anomalies. In this session we examine each phase of container security. We discuss the principle of minimal base images, using distroless or Alpine images instead of full Ubuntu images to reduce the attack surface from hundreds of packages to a handful. We implement container image scanning with tools like Trivy to identify known vulnerabilities in base images and application dependencies. We configure Docker security options including dropping all Linux capabilities and adding back only those required, applying seccomp profiles to restrict system calls, and running containers as non-root users. We examine container escape attacks and the defenses that prevent them, understanding the boundary between container isolation and full VM isolation.
key_concepts: Container isolation mechanisms: Linux namespaces (PID, network, mount, user) and cgroups (resource limits) sharing the host kernel
Container vs VM isolation: VMs have separate kernels (strong isolation); containers share the host kernel (weaker isolation but better efficiency)
Minimal base images: using distroless, Alpine, or scratch images to minimize packages and reduce vulnerability surface area
Image scanning: Trivy, Grype, or Snyk scanning base images and application dependencies for known CVEs
Image signing and verification: Docker Content Trust and Sigstore/Cosign for verifying image integrity and authenticity
Running as non-root: USER directive in Dockerfile, rootless Docker/Podman to eliminate root-level kernel interaction
Linux capabilities: dropping all capabilities (--cap-drop=ALL) and adding only required ones (--cap-add=NET_BIND_SERVICE)
seccomp profiles: restricting which system calls a container can make, blocking dangerous calls like mount, ptrace, and unshare
Container escape attacks: exploiting kernel vulnerabilities, misconfigured capabilities, or mounted host paths to break isolation
Container network security: network policies restricting container-to-container communication, encrypted overlay networks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a hardened Docker container: starting with a distroless base image, scanning with Trivy to verify zero critical vulnerabilities, running as non-root, dropping all capabilities, applying a custom seccomp profile, then demonstrating that a simulated attacker inside the container cannot escape or escalate privileges
setup_requirements: Docker Engine installed on Linux VM
Trivy or Grype for image scanning
Sample vulnerable and hardened container images
seccomp profile templates and documentation

review.value: VMs: each guest has its own kernel, hardware-enforced isolation via hypervisor. Containers: share the host kernel, isolation via namespaces and cgroups. A kernel vulnerability in a container environment affects all containers. Use VMs for multi-tenant isolation; containers for application packaging.

Trivy scan results show CVE ID, severity, package name, installed version, and fixed version. Integrate scanning into CI/CD: fail builds with critical vulnerabilities. Scan regularly because new CVEs are discovered daily for existing packages.

Default Docker containers have 14 capabilities. Drop all and add back only what is needed. Most web applications need zero capabilities. A web server binding to port 80 needs only CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE. Never use --privileged, which grants all capabilities including CAP_SYS_ADMIN.

Running Docker daemon and containers without root using user namespaces. The container root maps to an unprivileged host user. Even if the container is compromised and the attacker gains root inside, they have no privileges on the host. Podman runs rootless by default.

learning_objectives: Compare the security architectures of iOS and Android operating systems
Identify mobile-specific threats and attack vectors
Evaluate mobile device management solutions for enterprise security
Assess the security implications of mobile OS customization and rooting/jailbreaking
readings: Apple Platform Security Guide: iOS and iPadOS sections
Android Security Architecture Documentation
NIST SP 800-124 Rev. 2: Guidelines for Managing Mobile Devices

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile operating systems present unique security challenges that differ fundamentally from desktop and server environments. Mobile devices operate in hostile physical environments where they can be lost, stolen, or used on untrusted networks. They run third-party applications from app stores that may contain malware or privacy-violating code. They contain sensitive data including corporate email, authentication credentials, and personal information. And they often serve as the second factor in multi-factor authentication, making their compromise particularly dangerous. In this session we compare the security architectures of the two dominant mobile platforms. iOS implements a closed ecosystem with mandatory code signing, App Store review, hardware-backed Secure Enclave for key storage, and strict application sandboxing. Android provides a more open platform with application sandboxing via Linux permissions and SELinux, verified boot, Google Play Protect scanning, and hardware-backed keystore. We examine the security implications of platform choices: iOS's closed ecosystem provides stronger baseline security but less flexibility; Android's openness provides customization but creates fragmentation and delayed security updates across manufacturers. We discuss jailbreaking and rooting, understanding why users do it, the security implications, and how enterprises detect and respond to modified devices.
key_concepts: iOS security model: hardware root of trust, Secure Enclave for key storage, mandatory code signing, App Store review, and sandboxing
iOS Data Protection: file-level encryption with per-file keys tied to device passcode and hardware UID, four protection classes
Android security model: application sandboxing via Linux UID per app, SELinux enforcement, verified boot, and hardware-backed keystore
Android update fragmentation: manufacturer and carrier delays in security patches creating vulnerability windows across the ecosystem
Application sandboxing: each mobile app runs in its own process with restricted file system access and controlled inter-app communication
Mobile threat landscape: malicious apps, network-based attacks, device theft, phishing optimized for small screens, and SIM-based attacks
Jailbreaking (iOS) and rooting (Android): removing manufacturer restrictions, disabling security controls, and the resulting risk surface
Mobile Device Management: remote wipe, policy enforcement, app management, VPN configuration, and compliance checking
Mobile Application Management: containerizing corporate apps and data separately from personal apps on BYOD devices
Mobile app security: certificate pinning, secure local storage, biometric authentication APIs, and anti-tampering protections
lecture_notes.demonstration: Examining Android application permissions and sandboxing by decompiling an APK with JADX to identify requested permissions and hardcoded secrets, then demonstrating how MDM policies can restrict app installation, enforce encryption, and remotely wipe a lost device
setup_requirements: Android emulator with sample applications
JADX decompiler for APK analysis
iOS security architecture diagrams
MDM demonstration environment

review.value: Dedicated security coprocessor that manages cryptographic keys, performs biometric matching, and handles Apple Pay. Keys never leave the Secure Enclave and are not accessible to the application processor, even if iOS is fully compromised.

Every process and file on Android has an SELinux context. Even if an attacker exploits an application vulnerability, SELinux confines the process to its designated permissions. A camera app compromised by an attacker still cannot access SMS messages or contacts.

Removes manufacturer security controls, disables verified boot, allows unsigned code execution, and may disable SELinux. The device becomes vulnerable to any malware that gains access. Enterprise MDM solutions detect rooted/jailbroken devices and can block corporate access.

Enforce passcode policy, require device encryption, restrict app installation to approved list, configure VPN and Wi-Fi profiles, locate lost devices, remote wipe corporate data, check compliance (OS version, root status), and containerize work data.

learning_objectives: Collect volatile and non-volatile forensic evidence from Windows and Linux systems
Analyze file system artifacts, registry entries, and log files for investigation
Maintain chain of custody and evidence integrity during forensic collection
Use forensic tools to investigate common attack scenarios on operating systems
readings: File System Forensic Analysis (Carrier) Ch. 1-4
Windows Forensic Analysis Toolkit (Carvey) Ch. 1-3
NIST SP 800-86: Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques

lecture_notes.introduction: When a security incident occurs, the operating system contains a wealth of forensic evidence that can reveal what happened, when it happened, how the attacker gained access, what they did, and what data was compromised. Collecting and analyzing this evidence correctly is essential for incident response, legal proceedings, and preventing recurrence. OS forensics is the systematic examination of operating system artifacts to reconstruct events and establish facts. In this session we cover the fundamentals of OS forensic investigation on both Windows and Linux platforms. We begin with the order of volatility: collecting the most ephemeral evidence first (memory, network connections, running processes) before moving to more persistent artifacts (files, logs, registry). We examine key forensic artifacts on each platform: Windows prefetch files that record application execution history, NTFS timestamps that show file creation, modification, and access times, the Windows Event Log that records security events, and the Registry that preserves user activity and system configuration history. On Linux, we examine bash history, authentication logs, cron job configurations, and file system timestamps. We discuss the tools of the trade: FTK Imager for disk imaging, Volatility for memory analysis, and Timeline Explorer for chronological event reconstruction. Throughout, we emphasize the importance of evidence integrity: cryptographic hashing of all evidence, chain of custody documentation, and the use of write blockers to prevent accidental evidence modification.
key_concepts: Order of volatility: memory (most volatile), network connections, running processes, disk contents, logs, physical media (least volatile)
Memory forensics: capturing RAM with tools like FTK Imager or WinPMEM, analyzing with Volatility to find processes, network connections, and injected code
Disk imaging: creating bit-for-bit forensic copies with write blockers, verifying integrity with MD5/SHA-256 hash comparison
Windows forensic artifacts: Prefetch files, NTFS MFT timestamps, Shellbags, Recent Items, Jump Lists, and browser history
Windows Registry forensics: SAM (user accounts), NTUSER.DAT (user activity), SYSTEM (services and drivers), SOFTWARE (installed programs)
Linux forensic artifacts: /var/log/auth.log (authentication), .bash_history (commands), /etc/crontab (persistence), wtmp/btmp (login records)
File system timeline analysis: creating a chronological timeline of file creation, modification, and access across the entire file system
Evidence integrity: MD5 and SHA-256 hashing of all evidence, write blockers for physical media, documented chain of custody
Chain of custody: documenting who collected the evidence, when, how it was transported, where it was stored, and who accessed it
Anti-forensics: techniques attackers use to destroy evidence including log wiping, timestamp manipulation, and secure file deletion
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performing a forensic investigation of a simulated compromised Linux server: capturing memory with LiME, imaging the disk, analyzing bash history for attacker commands, examining auth.log for unauthorized access, creating a file system timeline to identify modified files, and documenting findings with proper chain of custody
setup_requirements: Compromised Linux VM with planted forensic artifacts
FTK Imager Lite or dc3dd for disk imaging
Volatility 3 for memory analysis
Timeline Explorer or log2timeline for timeline creation

review.value: Collect memory first (volatile, lost on power-off). Then capture network state (netstat, connections). Then running processes. Then image the disk. Then collect physical evidence. Memory contains running malware, encryption keys, and network connections that disappear on reboot.

C:\Windows\Prefetch\*.pf files record application name, run count, and last eight execution timestamps. Even if malware is deleted, the Prefetch file may persist, proving it was executed. Essential artifact for proving malware execution.

Create a unified timeline from MFT timestamps, event logs, prefetch data, and browser history. Sorting chronologically reveals the attack narrative: initial access time, lateral movement, privilege escalation, data access, and exfiltration. Gaps in the timeline may indicate anti-forensics.

Hash the evidence immediately after collection: sha256sum disk_image.dd. Record the hash in the chain of custody document. Any subsequent verification must produce the same hash. If the hash changes, the evidence has been modified and may be inadmissible.

class_schedule.topic: Practical Hardening Lab and Course Review
learning_objectives: Demonstrate comprehensive OS hardening skills on both Windows and Linux
Apply all course concepts to secure a system from default installation to production-ready state
Verify hardening effectiveness through vulnerability scanning and compliance checking
Synthesize operating system security knowledge for advanced coursework preparation
readings: Review all course materials and lab exercises
CIS Benchmark quick reference guides for Windows and Linux
Course summary and practical exam preparation guide

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session brings together all fourteen weeks of operating system security knowledge into a comprehensive practical exercise. Over the semester, you have built a deep understanding of how operating systems provide security at the kernel level, how Windows and Linux implement access controls and audit logging, how services should be hardened and monitored, how patch management and security baselines maintain security over time, and how containers and mobile platforms extend these concepts to modern deployment models. In this capstone session, you will take a freshly installed system and harden it from default to production-ready, applying every concept from the course. You will configure user management with least privilege, set file permissions appropriate for each directory, harden network services, apply security baselines, configure logging and auditing, and verify your work with vulnerability scanning. This exercise mirrors what you will do in your professional career: taking responsibility for the security of systems and being able to verify and prove that they meet security standards. The practical exam will follow a similar format, testing your ability to perform these tasks under time pressure with minimal reference materials. The operating systems security skills you have developed are foundational: every advanced course in penetration testing, incident response, and security architecture assumes you can look at a system and identify what is properly secured and what is not.
key_concepts: Comprehensive hardening workflow: OS installation, user management, file permissions, service configuration, network security, logging, patching, and verification
Defense in depth at the OS level: kernel security features, MAC policies, file permissions, service isolation, and logging working together
Verification methodology: vulnerability scanning, compliance checking, penetration testing, and log review to confirm hardening effectiveness
Documentation: recording all configuration changes, rationale, and verification results for audit and knowledge transfer
Continuous security: hardening is not a one-time event but an ongoing process of monitoring, patching, and responding to new threats
Cross-platform skills: ability to secure both Windows and Linux systems is essential in mixed enterprise environments
Container security integration: applying OS security principles to containerized workloads
Mobile security considerations: extending OS security concepts to mobile platforms
Forensic readiness: systems configured for both security and forensic investigation capability
Course progression: CY103 skills feed directly into advanced courses in penetration testing, incident response, and security architecture
lecture_notes.demonstration: Instructor-led walkthrough of a complete hardening exercise: taking a fresh Ubuntu Server installation through CIS Level 2 compliance using a combination of manual configuration, Ansible automation, and OpenSCAP verification, demonstrating the before-and-after vulnerability scan comparison
setup_requirements: Fresh Windows Server and Ubuntu Server VMs for hardening
CIS Benchmark reference guides
OpenSCAP and CIS-CAT scanning tools
Vulnerability scanner for before-and-after comparison

review.value: Step 1: Update and patch. Step 2: Configure user accounts and groups. Step 3: Set file permissions. Step 4: Harden network services. Step 5: Apply security baseline. Step 6: Configure logging and auditing. Step 7: Enable host firewall. Step 8: Scan and verify. Step 9: Document everything.

OS security is not isolated controls but an integrated system. Kernel security enables process isolation. Process isolation enables service sandboxing. Service hardening reduces attack surface. Logging detects attacks on the remaining surface. Patching closes newly discovered gaps. Each layer supports the others.

The practical exam provides a fresh system and a list of hardening requirements. You must configure the system to meet requirements, verify with scanning tools, and document your work. Time management is critical: patch first, then users, then services, then verify.

CS110 Programming for Security builds automation skills to script the hardening tasks you have learned. Advanced CY courses apply OS security knowledge to penetration testing (attacking weaknesses you now understand) and incident response (investigating the artifacts you now know where to find).

quizzes.topic: OS Architecture, Windows Security, and Linux Security

questions.question: A Linux server runs a web application as root. An attacker exploits an SQL injection vulnerability. Explain the security impact of running as root versus a dedicated non-privileged user, and list three specific hardening measures that would limit the blast radius even if the application is compromised.
questions.answer: Running as root gives the attacker full system access upon exploitation: reading /etc/shadow, installing rootkits, modifying system binaries, pivoting to other systems, and accessing all data on the server. Running as a non-privileged user (e.g., www-data) confines the attacker to that user's permissions: only files readable by www-data, no system modification, no access to other users' data. Three hardening measures: (1) SELinux/AppArmor policy confining the web process to only its required files, network ports, and system calls, preventing access even if the attacker escalates to root within the container. (2) Drop all Linux capabilities except CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE (if needed for port 80/443). Without CAP_SYS_ADMIN, the attacker cannot mount file systems, load kernel modules, or perform most privilege escalation techniques. (3) systemd ProtectSystem=strict with a read-only file system and private /tmp, preventing the attacker from writing malware to the system or using /tmp for staging.

rubric.key: Clear explanation of root vs non-privileged impact
Three valid hardening measures with technical detail
Demonstrates understanding of defense-in-depth at OS level

questions.question: Describe how an attacker uses Mimikatz to extract credentials from a Windows system, explain why this attack works at the OS architecture level, and describe two defenses that prevent or mitigate this attack.
questions.answer: Mimikatz reads the memory of the LSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service) process, which stores authentication credentials including NTLM hashes, Kerberos TGTs, and plaintext passwords (if WDigest is enabled). It works because LSASS must hold these credentials in memory to support single sign-on and network authentication. Mimikatz requires SYSTEM or local administrator privileges to read LSASS memory. Defense 1: Enable Credential Guard, which uses Virtualization-Based Security (VBS) to isolate LSASS in a Hyper-V protected container. Even code running as SYSTEM on the host cannot read the protected LSASS memory because the hypervisor enforces isolation at the hardware level. Defense 2: Disable WDigest authentication by setting the registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest\UseLogonCredential to 0. This prevents plaintext passwords from being cached in LSASS memory. NTLM hashes are still present but are less useful without Kerberos tickets.

rubric.key: Correct description of Mimikatz technique and LSASS role
Two valid defenses with technical mechanism explanation

questions.question: Explain the difference between Discretionary Access Control (file permissions) and Mandatory Access Control (SELinux) on Linux. Provide a specific scenario where DAC alone fails to prevent an attack but MAC succeeds.
questions.answer: DAC: file owner sets permissions (rwx). The root user bypasses all DAC permissions. If an attacker gains root through any vulnerability, DAC provides zero protection because root can read, write, and execute any file. MAC (SELinux): the kernel enforces security policy based on security contexts assigned to processes and files. Even root is constrained by policy. A process running as root with context httpd_t can only access files labeled httpd_sys_content_t regardless of DAC permissions. Scenario: A web server running as root has a remote code execution vulnerability. With DAC only: the attacker gains a root shell and can read /etc/shadow, install a backdoor in /usr/bin, and access the database configuration with credentials. With SELinux: the attacker gains a root shell inside the httpd_t context. SELinux denies access to /etc/shadow (shadow_t context), denies writing to /usr/bin (bin_t context), and restricts database access to only the port and socket allowed by policy. The root shell is effectively contained to web server operations.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between DAC and MAC
Correct explanation of root bypass in DAC
Specific scenario showing MAC preventing what DAC allows

quizzes.topic: User Management, File Systems, and Process Security

questions.question: A company discovers that a former employee's domain account was used to access sensitive file shares three weeks after their termination. Identify all the user management failures and describe the automated controls that should have been in place.
questions.answer: Failures: (1) Active Directory account not disabled upon termination. (2) No automated integration between HR system and IT for offboarding. (3) No regular review of active accounts against HR employment roster. (4) No monitoring for account usage by terminated employees. (5) No multi-factor authentication that would require the former employee's physical token. Automated controls: (1) HR-to-IT provisioning integration that automatically disables the AD account and revokes all group memberships when HR records termination. (2) Automated daily reconciliation comparing active AD accounts against HR employment database, flagging discrepancies. (3) SIEM alert for authentication from accounts flagged for termination review. (4) MFA requirement for all file share access, so even if the password is known, the physical second factor would prevent access. (5) Quarterly access review requiring managers to certify that all account holders in their team are current employees with appropriate access.

rubric.key: Identifies multiple user management failures
Describes automated offboarding controls
Addresses detection and prevention comprehensively

questions.question: A Linux system has the following permissions on /usr/bin/custom_app: -rwsr-xr-x 1 root root. The application has a buffer overflow vulnerability. Explain the security risk, the attack scenario, and three ways to mitigate the risk without removing the application.
questions.answer: The SUID bit (s in owner execute position) means any user executing this program runs it with root privileges. Combined with the buffer overflow: any local user can exploit the overflow to gain a root shell because the process is already running as root. Attack: unprivileged user triggers the buffer overflow, overwrites the return address, redirects execution to attacker-controlled shellcode or ROP chain, and spawns a root shell. Mitigations: (1) Remove SUID and use Linux capabilities instead: setcap with only the specific capability needed (e.g., cap_net_raw if it needs raw sockets). The application gets only the required privilege, not full root. (2) Apply an AppArmor or SELinux profile to the binary, confining it to only its required files and operations even if exploited with root privileges.
